What looks good
- Heels are down and you look stable under the bar the whole set.
- Bar path is consistent rep to rep.

Main fixes

- Foot/leg drive setup: Your feet are wide and far forward. Slide each foot back so your shins are closer to vertical and keep them closer to the bench when viewed from the front. Heels stay planted and drive through them without letting your butt lift.
- Elbow angle: You’re flaring out. Tuck to roughly 45 degrees to your torso during the descent to protect the shoulders and keep the lats engaged.
- Touch point and ROM: Lower until the bar gently touches high chest/just below the collarbones, then press—don’t stop short.
- Grip and wrists: Grip looks a bit relaxed with wrists extended. Squeeze the bar hard and stack wrist over elbow so your forearms are vertical at the bottom.

Mental cues

- “Feet back, heels flat, knees out.”
- “Big breath, brace the gut.”
- “Bend the bar—tuck elbows.”
- “Touch high chest, then drive up and slightly back.”
- “Eyes on one ceiling spot; re‑rack with locked elbows.”

{"answer": "Love the effort here—let’s dial a few things in so you can press heavier, safer.

What looks good
- You’re touching the bar to your chest each rep—nice full range of motion.
- Butt and upper back are staying on the bench; you’re locking out at the top.

Main fixes
- Wrist and grip: Your wrists are extended back and the bar sits in your fingers. Stack the bar over your forearm by setting it in the heel of your palm, wrap the thumb, and crush the bar. This reduces wrist strain and keeps force straight into the bar.
- Forearm angle/grip width: At the chest, your forearms look angled in. Widen your grip slightly so the forearms are vertical when the bar touches your chest. This improves bar path and power off the chest.
- Upper‑back tightness: Your chest looks a bit “flat.” Before unracking, pinch the shoulder blades together and use your upper back as a solid platform. Grip hard and “bend the bar” to keep lats/upper back tight throughout the set.
- Bar path and eye focus: At lockout the bar should be directly over your shoulder joints. Lower to the mid‑to‑lower chest, then press back toward that lockout point (an upside‑down J). Fix your eyes on a single spot on the ceiling and drive the bar back to that spot every rep to keep the path consistent.
- Leg drive and foot setup: Heels are up, which is fine, but make sure the feet don’t wander. Either keep feet flat or stay on the toes, push the knees out slightly, squeeze the glutes, and drive your legs into the floor as you initiate the press.
- Control at the chest: Touch the chest lightly—no bounce or sinking. Keep reps consistent to build true pressing strength.

Mental cues
- Knuckles to the ceiling; bar in the heel of the palm.
- Pinch shoulder blades and keep chest tall.
- Squeeze the bar—“bend it” to stay tight.
- Eyes on one ceiling spot; press back to that spot.
- “Legs on”—drive through the floor, glutes tight.
- Touch, don’t bounce; same touch point every rep.

{"answer": "Love that you filmed your bench—let’s dial it in!

What looks good
- Solid lockouts and a consistent touch around the lower chest.
- Closed grip with wrist wraps—nice and secure.

Main fixes
- Elbow angle: You’re flaring a bit wide on the descent. Drop your elbows to about 45° relative to your torso on the way down, then let them naturally flare a touch as you press through the sticking point. This keeps shoulders safer and the bar path more efficient.
- Upper‑back platform: Your shoulders look like they roll forward near the chest. Before unracking, squeeze your shoulder blades together and keep them pinned (scapular retraction/depression) throughout the set. Think of your upper back as the platform you press from.
- Feet and leg drive: Your right heel is up and your lower body looks a little loose. Either get both heels flat (regulation) or go up on your toes if that gives you more arch—just pick one and keep both feet planted and still. Drive your legs into the floor and keep your hips down so your glutes maintain contact with the bench.
- Unrack and bar path: It looks like you lift the bar up out of the hooks. Unrack by lifting out, not up, so you don’t lose your tight setup. Fix your eyes on one point on the ceiling; touch the bar, then “throw it back” toward that point for the slight upside‑down J path instead of straight up.
- Bracing and grip: Take a deep breath into your gut, puff your chest, squeeze the bar as hard as you can, and “bend the bar” to keep the upper back tight.
- Safety: If this is near max effort, have a spotter for the liftoff and rerack.

{"answer": "Awesome work getting this set on video—great way to level up fast.

What looks good
- You’re setting a solid arch and keeping your upper back on the bench most of the time.
- You’re taking the bar through a full range of motion down to the chest and back to lockout.

Main fixes
- Plant your feet. Your heels are up and your feet/knees shift during the set (“floppy feet”). Get four points of contact—head, upper back, glutes, and feet—locked in. I prefer flat feet; bring them back under you but keep the heels down, or if you stay on toes, wedge them hard and don’t let them move. This will give you better leg drive and overall tightness.
- Set your upper back tighter. I can see the shoulders creeping forward at the bottom. Before unracking, pinch the shoulder blades together and keep your chest “puffed up.” Grip the bar hard and “bend it” or “rip it in half” to keep the lats and upper back engaged the whole rep.
- Stack your wrists. Your wrists are extended back over the bar. Place the bar lower in the palm (over the heel of the hand), wrap the thumbs, and keep the wrist stacked over the elbow for a stronger press and healthier wrists.
- Clean up the bar path and elbow angle. On the way down you’re flaring a bit and the bar drifts. Lower to a consistent touch point on the lower chest/upper sternum with elbows about 45° from your torso, then press back slightly toward your eyes, not straight up.
- Unrack smoothly. Set your eyes under the bar so you can lift out, not up. If you have to shrug up to clear the hooks, drop the J-hooks one notch or use a spotter for the liftoff, then keep your hips and glutes down after the unrack.

Mental cues
- Feet flat and still; push the floor away.
- Big belly breath and brace; chest up.
- Pinch shoulder blades; bend the bar.
- Touch low chest; press back toward your eyes.
- Squeeze the bar—thumbs wrapped, wrists stacked.

What looks good

- Foot position/leg drive: Your feet are set out in front and your shins angle forward. Bring each foot back toward the bench so your shins are nearly vertical and keep the heels flat and planted. This lets you drive through the heels to reinforce your arch without your hips rising.
- Upper-back setup and arch: Your chest looks a bit flat on the bench. Before you unrack, pinch your shoulder blades together and plant your upper back firmly to create a stable platform and keep the chest high throughout the set.
- Unrack and bar path: Set up with your eyes directly under the bar. Unrack by lifting out, not up, then keep your eyes fixed on one spot on the ceiling and drive the bar back to that same point each rep to keep a consistent path.

Mental cues

- Heels down, feet back, knees out; don’t let the feet move.
- Big breath into your gut, brace hard, chest up.
- Pinch shoulder blades and stay on your upper back.

What looks good
- You’re touching the bar to your chest each rep and keeping a solid arch so the chest stays high.
- Upper back looks planted on the bench and your grip width is reasonable.

Main fixes
- Feet/leg drive: Your feet are splayed wide and look loose. Get them closer to the bench when viewed from the front, tuck them back so your shins are nearly vertical, and keep your heels flat and planted. Floppy feet kill tightness and leg drive.
- Butt contact: Your hips look like they’re hovering during the press. Keep your glutes down—use your legs to reinforce the arch, not to bridge your butt off the pad.
- Elbow angle: You’re flaring the elbows out. On the descent, drop the elbows to about 45 degrees relative to your torso to protect the shoulders and stay tight.
- Bar path: You appear to press straight up. Touch the chest, then drive the bar back toward the point on the ceiling you’re staring at—an upside‑down J path.
- Unrack/eye position: Set up with your eyes directly under the bar, lift the bar out (not up), lock the elbows, and fix your gaze on one spot on the ceiling so every rep touches the same point on your chest.

- Heels down, feet flat; knees close to the bench.
- Squeeze glutes but keep butt on the pad.

- Stare at one ceiling spot; don’t move your eyes.

- Upper‑back and arch: Your torso looks fairly flat. Before unracking, pinch your shoulder blades together and down and keep a small arch so your chest is high and stable.
- Leg drive: Your feet are a bit forward. Bring them back as far as you can while keeping heels flat and knees pushed out. Keep glutes squeezed and drive through the floor as the bar leaves your chest.

Mental cues

- Feet back, heels down, knees out; squeeze glutes and drive.

{"answer": "Awesome work getting after it! Here’s a quick form tune‑up for your bench press.

What looks good
- You’re touching the bar to your chest each rep and keeping your butt on the bench.
- You’ve got a solid arch, which helps keep the chest high.

Main fixes
- Plant your feet and use leg drive. Your heels look off the floor and the feet drift. Set your feet back so your shins are nearly vertical, then get your heels flat and keep them planted the whole set. This stops “floppy feet” and gives you a stronger base.
- Lock in the four points of contact. Head, upper back/shoulder blades, glutes, and feet should all stay planted. Pinch the shoulder blades together to create a stable platform.
